title: 6 0 Revision of Linear Algebra
body:
TOPIC 6 MATRICES
                6.0 REVISION OF LINEAR ALGEBRA


вҖў If A is an m Г— n matrix and B is an p Г— q matrix then the matrix product AB is
defined if n = p (inner dimensions match). The product is an m Г— q matrix (the outer
dimensions). The order of products matters so that, in general, AB 6= BA.
вҖў The identity matrix I serves as the вҖң1вҖқ for matrices, that is AI = IA = A.


вҖў The transpose of A (denoted by AT ) has the columns of A as its rows. If A is m Г— n
then AT is n Г— m.


вҖў A matrix is said to be symmetric if A = AT .


вҖў Given a square matrix A, the inverse of A (denoted by AвҲ’1 ) is another matrix with
the property that AAвҲ’1 = AвҲ’1 A = I.


вҖў AвҲ’1 exists if and only if det(A) 6= 0. In this case we say that the matrix A is invertible.
                                                    
            a b              вҲ’1      1         d вҲ’b

            c d                   ad вҲ’ bc вҲ’c a
вҖў For larger square matrices the inverse is calculated via row reduction using the operations

                             Ri = Ri Вұ ОұRj       and Ri вҶ” Rj .


These lecture notes are a revision of first year linear algebra covering matrices, inverses,
determinants, systems of linear equations, Gaussian Elimination and row reduction. If
you are rusty on this material you should have a look over your first year linear algebra
notes before we move on to the following lectures.

Matrices are simply rectangular arrays of numbers which may be manipulated as mathe-
matical objects.

                                            3
 Matrix division is not quite properly defined. The closest we can manage is to use the
concept of an inverse.

Given a square matrix A the inverse of A (denoted by AвҲ’1 ) is another matrix with the
property that AAвҲ’1 = I.
                                                           вҲ’1                             

techniques are possible.

Note that the above method for solving linear equations is NOT generally used. The
calculation of the inverse is numerically inefficient and the method fails completely when
there are infinite solutions. When finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors in the following
chapters we will always use Guassian Elimination to Echelon Form.

                                            5
 We close the lecture with the definitions of transpose and symmetry for matrices.

Definition: The transpose AT of a matrix A is defined as [AT ]ij = [A]ji .

AT is quite simply the matrix whose rows are the columns of A (and vice versa).

Example 5 Find AT if:
               
         3 1 вҲ’2
a) A =
         4 7 8
          пЈ«пЈ¶
         3
b) A = пЈӯ 6 пЈё
         9




                                                                                    F

Fact: If A is m Г— n then AT is n Г— m.

Fact: (AB)T = B T AT

Definition: A matrix A is said to be symmetric if A = AT .
The following are examples of symmetric matrices:

For a symmetric matrix the first row is the same as the first column, the second row is
the same as the second column... etc.

Example 6 Show that the matrix C = AT A is symmetric for any matrix A.
